Youth Employment Permitting
16

Senate Committee on Ways and Means Fiscal Year 2027 Budget Recommendations
335 SECTION 32. Chapter 149 of the General Laws is hereby amended by striking out
336 sections 86 to 89, inclusive, as so appearing, and inserting in place thereof the following 4
337 sections:-
338 Section 86. A person shall not employ a child, other than a child over the age of 14 who
339 has been granted an employment permit pursuant to section 87 when it is determined that the
340 welfare of the child will be better served through the granting of the permit, in any place of
341 employment, other than street trades described in section 69; provided, however, that a child 14
342 years of age or older in co-operative courses in a public school may be employed by a co-
343 operating establishment upon securing from the superintendent of schools a permit covering the
344 co-operating employment. Children between 14 and 16 years of age who possess the educational
345 qualifications set forth in section 1 of chapter 76 and are employed in private domestic service or
346 service on farms shall be required to secure a permit issued by the superintendent of schools
347 covering such employment. A person employing a minor 14 years of age or older shall procure
348 and keep on file, accessible to the minor, to supervisors of attendance of the town, to agents of
349 the department of elementary and secondary education, to agents of the department and to the
350 attorney general or the attorney general’s authorized agents or inspectors, the permit for
351 employment issued to the minor and shall keep a complete list of the names and ages of all
352 minors so employed.
353 The department shall provide for a process that allows for youth employees to submit
354 complaints of workplace rights violations.
355 All documents related to this section may be provided, generated, approved and signed in
356 an electronic format as approved by the department.
17

Senate Committee on Ways and Means Fiscal Year 2027 Budget Recommendations
357 Section 87. Each superintendent of schools or, where there is no superintendent of
358 schools, the school committee, shall designate a youth work permit coordinator.
359 An employment permit shall be issued only by the superintendent, or the superintendent’s
360 designee, or by the youth work permit coordinator of the town wherein the child to whom it is
361 issued resides during their employment or in the town wherein the child attends school or, if the
362 child resides outside the commonwealth, of the town wherein the child is to be employed;
363 provided, however, that no person authorized shall issue a permit for a child then in or about to
364 enter that person’s own employment or the employment of a firm or corporation of which that
365 person is a member, officer or employee, except when the child is employed by the school or
366 school district in which they are enrolled. Minors 17 years of age or older who can show
367 documented proof of a high school diploma or the equivalent thereof to the youth work permit
368 coordinator may be employed without the signature of the youth work permit coordinator or any
369 person so authorized.
370 A minor seeking an employment permit shall submit an application to the applicable
371 youth work permit coordinator. The youth work permit coordinator, either before or after the
372 minor receives an offer of employment, shall receive, examine, approve and file the following
373 duly-executed documents:
374 (i) the school record of a minor under 16 years of age, filled out and signed as provided in
375 section 88, except when such record may be waived thereunder, and evidence that a minor 16
376 years of age or older meets the requirements for the completion of the sixth grade of the public
377 schools of the town wherein the minor resides;
378 (ii) for a minor under 16 years of age, a certificate, signed within the previous 12 months,
379 by a school or family healthcare provider or by a healthcare provider appointed by the applicable
18

Senate Committee on Ways and Means Fiscal Year 2027 Budget Recommendations
380 school committee, stating that the minor has been examined by the healthcare provider and, in
381 the provider’s opinion, is in sound health; and
382 (iii) evidence of age, showing that the child is of the age required for the issuance of the
383 permit, which shall consist of at least 1 of the following:
384 (a) a birth certificate or a duly-attested transcript thereof made by a registrar of vital
385 statistics or other officer charged with the duty of recording births; or
386 (b) a religious record, or a duly-attested transcript thereof, showing the age of the child.
387 (c) If none of the aforesaid proofs of age is obtainable, and only in such case, the person
388 issuing employment permits may accept a passport or a duly-attested immigration record, or a
389 transcript thereof, showing the age of the child, or other official record of the child’s age;
390 provided, however, that it shall appear to the satisfaction of the person that the same is good and
391 sufficient evidence of the child’s age.
392 (d) If none of the above proofs of age is obtainable, and only in such case, the person
393 issuing employment permits may accept a record of age as given on the register of the school
394 which the child first attended in the commonwealth; provided, however, that such record was
395 kept for at least 2 years during the time when such child attended school.
396 (e) If none of the above proofs of age is obtainable, and only in such case, the person
397 issuing employment permits may accept the signed statement of the school healthcare provider
398 or of the healthcare provider appointed by the school committee stating that after examination it
399 is the opinion of such healthcare provider that the child is at least of the age required for the
400 issuance of the permit. Such healthcare provider statement shall be accompanied by a statement
401 signed by the child’s parent, guardian or custodian or, if the child has no parent, guardian or
402 custodian, by the signed statement of the next adult friend. Such signed statement shall contain
19

Senate Committee on Ways and Means Fiscal Year 2027 Budget Recommendations
403 the name, date and place of birth and residence of the child and shall certify that the parent,
404 guardian, custodian or next adult friend signing it is unable to produce any of the proofs of age
405 specified in this section. The person issuing employment permits may, before issuing such a
406 permit, require the parent, guardian, custodian or next adult friend of the child to appear and
407 approve in writing the issuance of such permit.
408 The superintendent of schools, the school committee or any other person authorized to
409 receive, examine, approve and file evidence of age shall not retain, against the will of such child
410 or the child’s parent or guardian, such evidence of age, for a longer time than is reasonably
411 necessary for making a copy, photostat or reasonable facsimile thereof, which shall be filed in
412 place of the original and the original shall be returned to such child or to the child’s parent or
413 guardian. A certificate relating to the age or place of birth of any child or to any other fact sought
414 to be established in relation to school attendance shall be issued, upon request, by a town clerk
415 and no fee shall be charged therefor by a town clerk or other official.
416 After an employer makes an offer of employment, the following shall be documented: a
417 pledge or promise, signed by the prospective employee and by the child’s parent or guardian, and
418 by the employer or by an authorized manager or superintendent, setting forth the character of the
419 specific employment, the number of hours per day during which the child is to be regularly
420 employed and the name and address of the employer, in which pledge or promise the employer
421 agrees to employ the child in accordance with this chapter which is summarized on the pledge or
422 promise form.
423 After an employer has made an offer of employment and completed the pledge or
424 promise required by this section, the employment permit shall be issued. The permit shall be
425 accessible electronically by the child, the child’s parent or guardian, the child’s school, the
20

Senate Committee on Ways and Means Fiscal Year 2027 Budget Recommendations
426 department of elementary and secondary education, the department and the office of the attorney
427 general.
428 The superintendent of schools, the youth work permit coordinator, or a person authorized
429 by them in writing, may revoke for cause the permit for employment of any child, except those
430 employed in private domestic service by a member of the child’s immediate family, or service as
431 a farm laborer in the employ of the child’s parent or legal guardian. Whenever a permit
432 authorizing employment of a child elsewhere than in their place of residence is held by them, the
433 superintendent of schools or youth work permit coordinator of the town of their employment
434 shall forthwith notify the superintendent of schools or youth work permit coordinator issuing the
435 permit of the child’s failure to comply with any relevant provisions of law.
436 All documents related to this section may be provided, generated, approved and signed in
437 an electronic format as approved by the department.
438 Section 88. The school record required by section 87 shall be provided by the principal or
439 teacher in charge of the school which the child last attended and shall be furnished only to a child
440 who, after due examination and investigation, is found to be entitled thereto. The school record
441 shall state: (i) the grade last completed by the child; (ii) the number of days during which the
442 child has attended school during the 12 months immediately preceding the time of application for
443 the school record; and (iii) the name, date of birth and residence of the child as shown on the
444 records of the school and the name of the parent, guardian or custodian. If the school record is
445 not obtainable from the school where the child last attended, the school record requirement may
446 be waived by the youth work permit coordinator.
447 No school record shall be issued or accepted and no employment permit shall be granted
448 unless the child possesses the educational qualifications described in section 1 of chapter 76;
21

Senate Committee on Ways and Means Fiscal Year 2027 Budget Recommendations
449 provided, however, that a child over 14 years of age who does not possess such qualifications
450 may be granted a limited employment permit good only during hours when school is not in
451 session.
452 No such school record shall be issued or accepted unless the child has attended the public
453 or other lawfully-approved schools for not less than 130 days after reaching the age of 13;
454 provided, however, that the school record may be accepted if the person has attended a public
455 day or other lawfully-approved school for not less than 7 years if, in the opinion of the
456 superintendent of schools, such person is mentally incapable of acquiring the educational
457 qualifications herein prescribed. The superintendent may suspend this requirement if the interests
458 of the child, as determined by the superintendent, will best be served by such suspension.
459 All documents related to this section may be provided, generated, approved and signed in
460 an electronic format as approved by the department.
461 Section 89. The employment permit required under this chapter shall be in a manner
462 prescribed by the department and shall contain the information deemed necessary by the
463 department, in coordination with the attorney general’s office. The permit shall certify that the
464 child named in the permit has been found to possess the educational qualifications described in
465 section 1 of chapter 76, except in the case of a limited permit, that all the papers required by
466 section 87 have been duly examined, approved and filed and all the conditions and requirements
467 for issuing an employment permit have been fulfilled. The permit shall state the grade last
468 completed by the child and shall be signed by the child in whose name the permit is to be issued.
469 The permit shall state the name of the employer and the nature of the employment in which the
470 permit authorizes the child to be employed. A permit shall bear a number, the date of its issue
471 and the signature of the person issuing it. No fee shall be exacted by a town clerk or other official
22

Senate Committee on Ways and Means Fiscal Year 2027 Budget Recommendations
472 for an employment permit or for any paper required by sections 87 to 95, inclusive. No duplicate
473 employment permit shall be issued until it shall appear to the satisfaction of the person
474 authorized to issue permits that the original has been lost. A record giving all the facts contained
475 on every employment permit issued shall be maintained together with the documents required
476 under section 87. A record shall also be kept of the names and addresses of all children to whom
477 permits have been refused, together with the names of the schools which the children attend and
478 the reasons for each refusal. Each of the aforesaid records and papers shall be preserved until
479 such children, if living, have reached the age of 18. The records and statistics concerning the
480 issuance of employment permits as may be prescribed by the department of elementary and
481 secondary education shall be kept and shall be open for inspection by the department, its officers
482 and agents. The blank permits and other papers required in connection with the issuing of
483 employment permits and educational certificates under this chapter shall be furnished to the local
484 school committees by the department, by which they shall be prepared after conference with the
485 department of elementary and secondary education and the approval of the forms thereof by the
486 attorney general. The permits, certificates and documents may bear such explanatory matter as
487 may be necessary to facilitate the enforcement of this chapter or to comply with any other
488 requirements as prescribed by law.
489 All documents related to this section may be provided, generated, approved and signed in
490 an electronic format as approved by the department.

Turning 22 Commission
532 SECTION 37. (a) There shall be a special commission to: (i) review current practices and
533 gaps in transitional planning services; (ii) prepare a comprehensive plan to address the complex
25

Senate Committee on Ways and Means Fiscal Year 2027 Budget Recommendations
534 and growing number and needs of persons with disabilities whose entitlement to services under
535 special education programs has terminated or will terminate as a result of such person’s
536 graduation from high school or attainment of age 22, hereafter referred to as transition-age
537 youth; and (iii) recommend solutions to improve transition planning services and outcomes for
538 transition-age youth and their families or guardians.
539 (b) The commission shall consist of: the secretary of health and human services, or a
540 designee, who shall serve as co-chair; the secretary of education, or a designee, who shall serve
541 as co-chair; 1 representative of the transitional advisory committee within the bureau of
542 transitional planning; the commissioner of the state vocational rehabilitation agency within the
543 executive office of labor and workforce development, or a designee; the commissioner of
544 MassAbility, or a designee; the commissioner of the department of developmental services, or a
545 designee; the commissioner of the department of mental health, or a designee; 1 member of the
546 senate appointed by the senate president; 1 member of the house of representatives appointed by
547 the speaker of the house of representatives; 1 member appointed by the minority leader of the
548 senate; 1 member appointed by the minority leader of the house of representatives; the chairs of
549 the joint committee on children, families and persons with disabilities; 1 member representing
550 The Arc of Massachusetts; 1 member representing the Massachusetts Down Syndrome Congress;
551 1 member representing the Federation for Children with Special Needs; and 3 members
552 appointed by the governor, 1 of whom shall represent a program that serves transition-age youth,
553 1 of whom shall be a transition-age youth and 1 of whom shall be a caregiver or family member
554 of a recent transition-age youth with high support needs.
555 (c) The plan shall include, but not be limited to, strategies and recommendations for: (i)
556 improving and strengthening habilitative services pursuant to sections 12A through 12C,
26

Senate Committee on Ways and Means Fiscal Year 2027 Budget Recommendations
557 inclusive, of chapter 71B of the General Laws for a person with disabilities whose entitlement to
558 services under special education programs has terminated or will terminate as a result of such
559 person’s graduation from high school or attainment of age 22; (ii) improving, enhancing and
560 modernizing transitional planning and services for persons with disabilities who: (A) are eligible
561 for adult services through the department of developmental services, department of mental health
562 or MassAbility pursuant to existing eligibility processes, including, but not limited to, referrals
563 under chapter 688 of the acts of 1983 or applications by the individual or family; (B) are
564 assessed to be prioritized for residential placement through the department of developmental
565 services; (C) require intensive staffing for complex behavioral or complex medical needs through
566 the department of developmental services; and (D) require services from 1 or more member
567 agencies of the transitional advisory committee; (iii) enhancing and improving coordination,
568 communication and data sharing across local education agencies, the department of
569 developmental services, department of mental health, MassAbility, individuals and their families
570 or guardians to support informed, person-centered transition planning including, but not limited
571 to, education on adult service systems, eligibility processes and available pathways to
572 employment and independence; (iv) improving and modernizing the referral program established
573 through said chapter 688 of the acts of 1983 to increase transparency, advance planning and
574 communication between all involved parties; (v) facilitating information sharing between
575 members of the bureau of transitional planning with the goal of serving transition-age youth and
576 their families or guardians prior to, during and after their transition to adult services; (vi)
577 expanding competitive integrated employment opportunities for transition-age youth with
578 disabilities, consistent with federal Employment First policies and the Workforce Innovation and
579 Opportunity Act; (vii) limiting or eliminating the number of transition-age youth who do not
27

Senate Committee on Ways and Means Fiscal Year 2027 Budget Recommendations
580 have a residential placement, if required by their transition plan, identified at least 6 months
581 before their transition to adult services and ensuring that there is a clear process for transition-
582 age youth for whom an alternative placement is not immediately available; (viii) increasing
583 options and opportunities for transition-age youth found eligible for residential supports to
584 maintain meaningful connections with their families or guardians, including through innovative
585 housing models, cross-agency collaboration and improved use of existing resources, while
586 accounting for and considering workforce capacity, clinical needs and housing availability; (ix)
587 addressing any unique transition challenges for transition-age youth who are leaving the care of
588 the department of children and families; (x) promoting, when possible, geographic equity in
589 services provided across the commonwealth including, but not limited to, habilitative services,
590 residential living opportunities, employment and day program supports and family supports; (xi)
591 establishment of regular review processes within the bureau of transitional planning regarding
592 the efficacy and results of any transition-age services provided by those same member
593 organizations; (xii) modernizing and updating existing reporting and transition planning data
594 collection processes and requirements by local education agencies and the department of
595 elementary and secondary education pursuant to the Individuals with Disabilities Education Act;
596 and (xiii) identifying evidence-based transition practices that improve postsecondary
597 employment and education outcomes for students with disabilities, including early engagement
598 with vocational rehabilitation services, participation in pre-employment transition services,
599 work-based learning experiences and career and technical education opportunities.
600 (d) In making its recommendations, the commission shall consider practices related to
601 communication, education and awareness efforts including, but not limited to: (i) the frequency
602 of transparent agency communication between relevant agencies and transition-age youth and the
28

Senate Committee on Ways and Means Fiscal Year 2027 Budget Recommendations
603 families and guardians of transition-age youth; (ii) the amount of notification time given for
604 transition plans to be developed and finalized; and (iii) raising awareness of programs,
605 examining outreach practices and the efficacy of services available prior to, during and after the
606 transition process to adult services.
607 (e) The report shall include recommendations for: (i) modernizing and strengthening
608 habilitative services and transitional planning services for persons with disabilities pursuant to
609 sections 12A through 12C, inclusive, of chapter 71B; and (ii) interagency data sharing and
610 transition outcome tracking across education, vocational rehabilitation and adult service systems,
611 including metrics related to employment, postsecondary enrollment and independent living
612 outcomes for persons with disabilities.
613 (f) The commission shall submit its report and recommendations, together with drafts of
614 legislation, if any, to the executive office of labor and workforce development, the executive
615 office of education, the executive office of health and human services, the clerks of the senate
616 and house of representatives, the senate and house committees on ways and means, the joint
617 committee on education and the joint committee on children, families and persons with
618 disabilities not later than September 30, 2027.

Massachusetts School Building Authority Commission
632 SECTION 39. (a) For the purposes of this section, “authority” shall mean the
633 Massachusetts School Building Authority established in section 1A of chapter 70B of the
634 General Laws.
635 (b) There shall be a special commission to study and make recommendations regarding:
636 (i) the capacity of the authority to meet the needs of current and future school facility projects;
637 and (ii) the racial, ethnic, socioeconomic and geographic equity of the grant funding formula to
638 ensure that all communities may participate in the maintenance assessment program established
639 in section 16 of chapter 70B of the General Laws.
640 (c) The commission shall consist of: the senate and house chairs of the joint committee
641 on education, who shall serve as co-chairs; 1 member to be appointed by the senate president; 1
642 member to be appointed by the speaker of the house of representatives; 1 member to be
643 appointed by the minority leader of the senate; 1 member to be appointed by the minority leader
644 of the house of representatives; the secretary of education, or a designee; the commissioner of
645 elementary and secondary education, or a designee; the executive director of the Massachusetts
646 School Building Authority, or a designee; 2 members to be appointed by the governor, 1 of
30

Senate Committee on Ways and Means Fiscal Year 2027 Budget Recommendations
647 whom shall be a representative of a gateway city with knowledge of municipal finance and 1 of
648 whom shall be an individual with demonstrated expertise in the design and construction of cost-
649 effective school buildings; 2 members to be appointed by the state treasurer, 1 of whom shall be
650 an individual with demonstrated expertise in the design and construction of green buildings and 1
651 of whom shall be an individual with demonstrated expertise in public health and indoor
652 environmental quality in school buildings; 1 member from the Massachusetts Association of
653 School Superintendents, Inc.; 1 member from the Massachusetts Association of School
654 Committees, Inc.; 1 member from the Massachusetts Association of Vocational Administrators,
655 Inc.; 1 member from the Massachusetts chapter of the American Institute of Architects; 1
656 member from the Massachusetts Facilities Administrators Association, Inc.; 1 member from the
657 Massachusetts Teachers Association; 1 member from the American Federation of Teachers,
658 Massachusetts; 1 member from the Massachusetts Taxpayers Foundation, Inc.; 1 member from
659 the Massachusetts Municipal Association, Inc.; and 1 member from the Massachusetts Building
660 Trades Council.
661 (d) The commission shall investigate and make recommendations on: (i) the current and
662 future need to renovate and rebuild school facilities, availability of adequate state and local
663 resources and the extent to which current state funding for the authority may need to be adjusted
664 to meet this need; (ii) the authority’s existing grant formula and potential modifications to the
665 formula to ensure fairness and equity for all communities; (iii) the alignment of the authority’s
666 construction cost reimbursement rate relative to the actual cost of construction and other
667 authority policies that impact eligible project costs for reimbursement; (iv) incentive percentage
668 points including, but not limited to, how incentive percentage points are calculated for
669 municipalities that qualify for 80 per cent reimbursement; (v) reimbursement policies for
31

Senate Committee on Ways and Means Fiscal Year 2027 Budget Recommendations
670 regional technical and vocational schools and ways to ensure affordability for all member
671 communities; (vi) reimbursement policies to assist the commonwealth in achieving its
672 environmental and greenhouse gas emissions reduction goals; (vii) reimbursement policies that
673 support healthy school facilities for students and staff; (viii) if the authority should be authorized
674 to spend money on equipment or be limited to funding education structures; (ix) if the authority
675 should add incentives for educational spaces approved under chapter 74 of the General Laws in
676 programs that align to labor market demand; (x) strategies for addressing facilities’ needs of
677 educational collaboratives; and (xi) any other issues affecting the ability of all students to attend
678 school in high quality, accessible, safe, healthy and green school buildings.
679 (e) The authority and other state agencies shall make available to the commission any
680 documents, data or materials in a timely manner when requested by the commission. The
681 authority shall provide staff to assist the commission in completing its obligations under this
682 section.
683 (f) The commission shall submit a report of its findings and recommendations to the
684 clerks of the senate and house of representatives, the joint committee on education and the senate
685 and house committees on ways and means not later than June 1, 2027.
Sheriff Funding Reporting
686 SECTION 40. (a) Notwithstanding any general or special law to the contrary, for fiscal
687 years 2027 through 2031, inclusive, the executive office for administration and finance shall
688 impose hiring and spending controls to restrict the number of employees for each sheriff’s office
689 and prevent spending in excess of appropriation. Such controls shall include, but not be limited
690 to, restrictions limiting the total number of employees at each office and procedures for any large
32

Senate Committee on Ways and Means Fiscal Year 2027 Budget Recommendations
691 capital expenditures; provided, however, that the executive office for administration and finance
692 may permit waivers from hiring controls for demonstrated public safety needs.
693 (b) Notwithstanding any general or special law to the contrary, for fiscal year 2027 and
694 consistent with current law, including, but not limited to, sections 26 and 27 of chapter 29 of the
695 General Laws, no sheriff’s office shall spend into deficiency; provided, however, that if a
696 sheriff’s office requires supplemental funding, said sheriff’s office shall submit sufficient
697 documentation of the need to spend into deficiency to the executive office for administration and
698 finance and the comptroller’s office prior to any supplemental budget being filed by the governor
699 to address such need.
700 (c) If the secretary of administration and finance determines that a sheriff’s office has
701 concluded fiscal year 2027 in deficiency without providing sufficient documentation of the
702 deficiency, or has not complied with the controls imposed pursuant to subsection (a), the
703 secretary shall place such sheriff’s office under the oversight of the sheriff fiscal oversight
704 council established in subsection (d).
705 (d)(1) There shall be within the executive office of public safety and security a sheriff
706 fiscal oversight council. The council shall oversee the finances of any sheriff’s office made
707 subject to its oversight under this section.
708 (2) The council shall consist of the secretary of public safety and security, who shall
709 serve as co-chair; the secretary of administration and finance, who shall serve as co-chair; and 3
710 members to be appointed by the governor, 2 of whom shall have experience in the operation of
711 correctional facilities and 1 of whom shall have experience in state government finance. The
712 oversight council shall meet and hire staff as it deems necessary to ensure proper oversight of
713 sheriff operations and finances.
33

Senate Committee on Ways and Means Fiscal Year 2027 Budget Recommendations
714 (3) The council, in carrying out this section, shall have access to all records, documents,
715 accounts, correspondence and any other data and material that is maintained by the sheriffs’
716 offices or other state agencies that the council deems necessary. Upon being subject to oversight
717 of the council and not less than monthly thereafter, each sheriff subject to the council’s
718 governance shall provide the council with information on the status of the revenues and expenses
719 for their office’s operating budget and on the status of revenues, contracting, procurement and
720 authorized and actual spending for any capital projects. Such reports shall provide itemized year-
721 to-date expenditures and detailed projections for anticipated expenses for the remainder of the
722 fiscal year and be in a form prescribed by the council. The council shall submit such monthly
723 reports to the secretary of public safety and security, the secretary of administration and finance,
724 the inspector general, the clerks of the senate and house of representatives, the chairs of the
725 senate and house committees on ways and means and the chairs of the joint committee on public
726 safety and homeland security.
727 (4) The council may set financial management metrics to ensure the fiscal and
728 operational integrity of sheriff operations. The council may formulate and recommend a plan for
729 a sheriff’s office to stabilize and strengthen its finances, management, operations and asset
730 conditions with a focus on maintaining activities specifically required by statute, case law or
731 court order while limiting cost growth year over year and ensuring total annual expenditures do
732 not exceed annual state appropriations.
733 (5) If a sheriff’s office fails to meet any financial management metrics or the
734 requirements of a recommended plan prescribed under clause (4), or if the sheriff’s office’s
735 failure to comply with subsection (b) demonstrates an inability to appropriately manage the fiscal
736 and operational integrity of the office, as determined by the council, the council shall take action
34

Senate Committee on Ways and Means Fiscal Year 2027 Budget Recommendations
737 to ensure the fiscal and operational integrity of such sheriff’s office, which may include, but shall
738 not be limited to: (i) appointing a receiver to take operational and managerial control of the
739 applicable sheriff’s office; provided, however, that such receiver shall have experience in the
740 operation of correctional facilities; (ii) directing the comptroller not to certify any amounts for
741 payment from a sheriff’s office; and (iii) prohibiting a sheriff’s office from incurring any new
742 expenditures or obligations without prior approval of the council for a period of time to be
743 determined by the council.
